questions.question: What is Grafana k6 and who maintains it?
questions.answer: Grafana k6 is an open source load and performance testing tool originally created by Load Impact and now maintained by Grafana Labs. Scripts are written in JavaScript; the execution engine is compiled in Go for high efficiency. In the testflow-k6 project, k6 validates SLOs for the TestFlow application at localhost:5050 (Docker image qaschool/testflow).
keyPoints: Open source — Grafana Labs since Load Impact acquisition
Focus: load, stress, spike, soak, and smoke for APIs and browser
JS scripts + Go runtime — low CPU/RAM per VU
Modern alternative to JMeter/Gatling for CI pipelines
testflow-k6: full suite against TestFlow API and /web pages
followUps: Is k6 free or does it require a license?
What is the difference between k6 OSS and k6 Cloud?

questions.question: What types of testing was k6 designed for?
questions.answer: k6 was designed for performance testing: smoke (availability), load (expected traffic), stress/breakpoint (capacity limit), spike (sudden peaks), and soak/endurance (prolonged load). It also covers browser and HTTP, WebSocket, and gRPC protocols. In testflow-k6 each type has a dedicated scenario: api-health, mixed-traffic, api-breakpoint, api-spike, and api-endurance.
keyPoints: Smoke: fast availability and baseline latency gate
Load: sustained traffic — validates SLOs under normal load
Stress/spike/soak: degradation, recovery, and temporal drift
Does not replace functional tests — complements the pyramid
Scenarios mapped in scripts/scenario-catalog.mjs
followUps: When to use load vs stress in practice?
Is k6 suitable for security testing (DDoS)?

questions.question: Why does k6 use JavaScript for scripts but Go in the engine?
questions.answer: JavaScript is familiar to QAs and front-end developers, enabling readable scripts and logic reuse. The Go engine runs thousands of VUs as lightweight goroutines, avoiding JVM or heavy thread overhead. The JS script runs in an isolated runtime per VU; HTTP requests are made by the native engine. In testflow-k6, ES module helpers (lib/http.js, auth.js) leverage import/export without sacrificing performance.
keyPoints: JS: low learning curve, adjacent npm ecosystem
Go: efficient concurrency — thousands of VUs on a modest machine
Separation: script (logic) vs runtime (I/O and metrics)
init context runs JS once; VU context repeats default function
Project uses ES modules with k6 v0.47+
followUps: Does k6 support TypeScript natively?
Why not Python like Locust?

questions.question: What is the difference between load, stress, spike, and soak testing?
questions.answer: Load simulates expected production traffic and validates SLOs under normal load — in the project, mixed-traffic with load profile. Stress/breakpoint pushes beyond normal to find degradation — api-breakpoint with degraded_responses metric. Spike applies a sudden burst (5→100 VUs) — api-spike with spike_failures. Soak maintains stable load for a long period — api-endurance with soak_latency and K6_SOAK_MINUTES.
keyPoints: Load: SLOs under typical traffic — mixed-traffic, api-auth, api-users
Stress: ramp to 100+ VUs — tolerates more relaxed thresholds
Spike: 10s peak — validates recovery after burst
Soak: 30 min steady (reducible locally) — detects memory leaks
Profiles in config/profiles.js; thresholds in config/thresholds.js
followUps: Which profile to use before a release?
How to document breakpoint results for ops?

questions.question: Does k6 replace functional E2E tests? Why or why not?
questions.answer: No. k6 validates behavior under load — latency, error rate, and throughput — while E2E (Cypress/Playwright in the TestFlow ecosystem) validates functional flows and UI. An endpoint can pass E2E with 1 user and fail under 50 VUs. testflow-k6 complements testflow-cypress/playwright with the same endpoints and credentials, but focuses on aggregated SLOs via thresholds.
keyPoints: E2E: functional correctness, selectors, UI regression
k6: performance, concurrency, degradation under pressure
Same BASE_URL and DEMO_EMAIL — environment alignment
Thresholds mirror functional budgets where possible
k6 browser (login-page) measures page load, does not replace E2E suite
followUps: When does a k6 check replace a Cypress assert?
How to prioritize investment between E2E and load?

questions.question: What is a Virtual User (VU) in k6?
questions.answer: A VU (Virtual User) simulates a concurrent user executing the default function in a loop until the test ends. Each VU has an isolated JS context and makes real HTTP requests against the system under test. The number of VUs is controlled by the executor (e.g., ramping-vus with stages). In api-health smoke, the smoke profile ramps to 2 VUs; in spike, it jumps from 5 to 100 VUs in 10 seconds.
keyPoints: 1 VU ≈ 1 concurrent user running the script
VUs run in parallel — not sequential
Executor defines how many VUs exist and when they ramp
Each iteration = one complete default function execution
CI uses ci profile with up to 5 VUs for a fast gate
followUps: How many VUs can a local machine handle?
VU vs JMeter thread — practical difference?

questions.question: What is the difference between an iteration and an HTTP request?
questions.answer: An iteration is one complete execution of the default function by a VU — it may contain multiple HTTP requests, sleeps, and checks. Metrics like http_reqs count requests; iterations counts how many times the VU ran the script. In the login-flow journey, one iteration includes GET login.html, POST /api/auth/login, and GET dashboard — three requests, one iteration.
keyPoints: Iteration: complete script cycle per VU
Request: each individual http.get/post
Built-in metrics: iterations, http_reqs, http_req_duration
Journeys with group() still count 1 iteration per loop
Throughput = requests/s; iterations/s = VU pace
followUps: How to correlate iterations with think time?
http_reqs vs http_req_duration — which to prioritize?

questions.question: Does k6 support WebSockets, gRPC, and browser?
questions.answer: Yes. k6 has native modules for WebSockets (k6/ws), gRPC (k6/net/grpc), and browser (k6/browser with Chromium). testflow-k6 focuses on HTTP REST and browser: API scenarios cover /health, /api/users, and /api/auth/login; login-page.js uses k6/browser with data-testid. WebSockets and gRPC are not in the current suite, but the architecture supports extension.
keyPoints: HTTP: project core — lib/http.js and lib/auth.js
Browser: scenarios/browser/login-page.js — shared-iterations, 2 VUs
WebSockets/gRPC: supported by k6, not implemented in TestFlow
Browser requires native k6 v0.52+ — does not run in default slim image
Browser module measures browser_page_load and browser_lcp
followUps: How to test WebSocket in k6?
Does browser module run in testflow-k6 CI?

questions.question: What are SLOs in the context of performance testing?
questions.answer: SLO (Service Level Objective) defines measurable quality targets — e.g., 99% of requests with p95 below 1500 ms and error rate below 1%. In k6, SLOs become thresholds that determine test pass/fail (exit code). testflow-k6 centralizes SLOs in config/thresholds.js, aligned with budgets from functional Cypress/Playwright tests.
keyPoints: SLO: quantitative objective — latency, error, throughput
Threshold: k6 expression that fails the test if violated
smokeThresholds: p(95)<1500, checks>99%, http_req_failed<0.5%
load/stress/soak have progressively more tolerant profiles
SLOs per endpoint via tags: endpoint:health, endpoint:auth_login
followUps: SLO vs SLA — what is the difference?
How to negotiate SLO with product owner?

questions.question: How does k6 complement Cypress and Playwright in TestFlow?
questions.answer: Cypress and Playwright validate functionality and UX with few users; k6 validates whether the same backend handles real concurrency. testflow-k6 reuses BASE_URL (localhost:5050), DEMO_EMAIL/DEMO_PASSWORD credentials, and endpoints.web paths — same qaschool/testflow app. Latency thresholds mirror functional expectations. k6 browser (login-page) covers Core Web Vitals without duplicating the full E2E suite.
keyPoints: Same target: TestFlow app — consistency across suites
Cypress/Playwright: functional regression per PR
k6 smoke: performance gate in CI (.github/workflows/k6.yml)
Load/stress: manual or workflow_dispatch — does not block PR
HTTP journeys simulate flows similar to E2E without browser
followUps: Should k6 run on every PR or only smoke?
How to share selectors between Playwright and k6 browser?

questions.question: Explain the architecture: JS script, Go runtime, VU goroutines.
questions.answer: The developer writes JavaScript scripts exporting options and a default function. The k6 binary (Go) parses the script in the init context, allocates VUs as lightweight goroutines, and executes the default function in a loop in the VU context. HTTP requests are made by the native Go engine — not the JS runtime — maximizing throughput. Metrics are aggregated centrally and evaluated against thresholds at the end.
keyPoints: Script layer: test logic in JS (ES modules in the project)
Go runtime layer: scheduling, HTTP, metrics, thresholds
1 goroutine per VU — efficient horizontal scalability
init: imports, options, setup run once globally
VU context: default function repeats until duration/stages end
followUps: What happens if the JS script throws an exception?
Does k6 scale horizontally across machines?

questions.question: Why is k6 more resource-efficient than JMeter?
questions.answer: JMeter uses JVM with heavy threads and GUI/protocol plugin overhead; k6 uses Go goroutines with minimal footprint per VU. k6 scripts are versionable code (Git) without XML; startup is instant compared to headless JMeter. In testflow-k6 CI, smoke runs in ~1 min with 3–5 native VUs — viable on ubuntu-latest without a dedicated cluster.
keyPoints: Goroutines vs JVM threads — much lower memory per VU
Single Go binary vs JVM + JMeter plugins
Textual JS scripts — review in PR, no GUI
Native CI integration — exit code, JSON summary, thresholds
JMeter still wins for legacy protocols and mature ecosystem
followUps: How many VUs can k6 run vs JMeter on the same machine?
When is JMeter still the better choice?

questions.question: What happens when a VU completes the default function?
questions.answer: After the default function returns, k6 immediately starts a new iteration on the same VU — in a continuous loop until the test ends (end of stages or duration). sleep() inside the function is part of the iteration. In api-health smoke, each iteration calls getHealth(), getUsers(), and login() with sleeps between them, repeating until ramp-down zeroes the VUs.
keyPoints: Automatic loop: VU does not 'die' after one iteration
Duration controlled by ramping-vus executor stages
gracefulRampDown lets active iterations finish
setup() runs once before VUs; teardown after all
Iterations/s depends on script duration + think time
followUps: How to limit total number of iterations?
shared-iterations vs ramping-vus — when to use each?

questions.question: How does init context vs VU context work in k6?
questions.answer: Init context runs once per script: imports, options definition, custom metrics, and global helper functions. VU context runs the default function repeatedly — each VU has an isolated copy of local variables. Code at module global scope runs in init; code inside the default function runs per iteration. In testflow-k6, Trend/Rate/Counter are created in init; login() is called per iteration in VU context.
keyPoints: Init: one execution — loads ES modules, defines options
VU: N parallel executions — local state per goroutine
Mutable globals in init are shared (caution!)
setup() is init-like — runs once before VUs
Project: getProfile() read in init; HTTP calls in VU context
followUps: Can I share a token between VUs without setup()?
Why is mutating globals in VU context dangerous?

questions.question: Does k6 support ES modules (`import/export`)?
questions.answer: Yes, since k6 v0.47+ with experimental ES module support. testflow-k6 uses import/export in all scripts: config/environments.js exports BASE_URL; lib/http.js exports getHealth(); scenarios import helpers. Internal bundler resolves relative dependencies. CommonJS (require) also works, but the project standardized ES modules for alignment with the modern JS ecosystem.
keyPoints: import/export between config/, lib/, scenarios/, journeys/
export { handleSummary } reuses lib/summary.js
Relative paths: '../../config/profiles.js'
No node_modules — only local project modules
scenario-catalog.mjs uses .mjs for Node build scripts
followUps: Does k6 resolve external npm packages?
How to organize imports in a monorepo?

questions.question: What is `handleSummary` and when is it called?
questions.answer: handleSummary is a hook exported by the script, invoked once at the end of the test with a data object containing all aggregated metrics. It returns a map of outputs — stdout, JSON files, etc. In testflow-k6, lib/summary.js implements buildSummary(): prints a console summary and writes results/summary-latest.json for CI artifacts. Scenarios do export { handleSummary }.
keyPoints: Called after last VU — final aggregated data
data.metrics: http_req_duration, checks, custom metrics
buildSummary generates stdout + timestamped JSON
CI uses --summary-export=results/summary.json in workflow
npm run test:report consumes summaries for HTML
followUps: handleSummary vs --out json — difference?
How to send summary to Slack in the hook?

questions.question: How does k6 handle concurrency between VUs?
questions.answer: Each VU is an independent Go goroutine running the script in real parallel — there is no central request queue. HTTP requests from different VUs hit the server simultaneously. k6 does not synchronize VUs with each other; coordination requires setup() returning read-only data or external services. In mixed-traffic, dozens of VUs fire health, users, and static pages concurrently.
keyPoints: Real parallelism — genuine pressure on the SUT
No lock between VUs — each loops independently
setup() returns JSON serialized to all VUs
Race conditions in the SUT are exactly what we want to expose
Spike test: 100 simultaneous VUs in 10 seconds
followUps: How to synchronize VUs for an exact burst?
k6 open-model vs closed-model — difference?

questions.question: How do you install k6 on macOS, Linux, and Windows?
questions.answer: macOS: brew install k6. Linux: official apt/deb repository (dl.k6.io) or snap. Windows: winget install k6 or Chocolatey. CI in .github/workflows/k6.yml installs via apt on ubuntu-latest. Alternative without local install: Docker grafana/k6:1.0.0 via scripts/run-k6.sh, which detects missing k6 and runs a container automatically.
keyPoints: Homebrew, apt, winget — official Grafana installers
CI version: latest stable from deb repository
Browser module: k6 v0.52+ native — default Docker image does not include it
run-k6.sh: Docker fallback with volume mounted at /scripts
Custom image testflow-k6-k6-influx:local for InfluxDB output
followUps: How to pin k6 version in CI?
Does k6 browser work in Docker?

questions.question: What is the difference between native k6 and the `grafana/k6` Docker image?
questions.answer: Native k6 uses a local binary — ideal for browser module, web dashboard, and fast debugging. The grafana/k6 image runs in an isolated container, mounting the project at /scripts — useful when k6 is not installed or for reproducibility. run-k6.sh chooses native if available; otherwise Docker with BASE_URL adjusted to host.docker.internal:5050. The k6-influx image includes the xk6-output-influxdb extension.
keyPoints: Native: browser, K6_DASHBOARD, lower startup latency
Docker: grafana/k6:1.0.0 — no browser, API tests OK
Compose profile monitoring: k6-influx service + InfluxDB
BASE_URL rewrite: localhost → host.docker.internal in Docker
GitHub Actions CI uses native k6 installed via apt
followUps: When to prefer Docker even with k6 installed?
How to build the k6-influx image locally?

questions.question: What is `scripts/run-k6.sh` in testflow-k6?
questions.answer: Bash wrapper that centralizes k6 execution: loads .env, configures InfluxDB output (K6_INFLUXDB=1), web dashboard (K6_DASHBOARD=true), chooses native k6 vs Docker vs docker compose monitoring profile, and injects -o xk6-influxdb when monitoring is active. Normalizes BASE_URL for container (host.docker.internal or testflow:5050). Single entry point for npm scripts and manual runs.
keyPoints: Loads .env and .env.influx.cloud if K6_INFLUXDB_TARGET=cloud
build_k6_args injects -o xk6-influxdb after 'k6 run'
Fallback: native → compose k6-influx → docker run
Dashboard: K6_WEB_DASHBOARD_EXPORT for HTML in results/
Used indirectly by npm run test:smoke, test:load:*, etc.
followUps: How to run smoke via run-k6.sh manually?
What does K6_INFLUXDB_TARGET=auto do?

questions.question: Which environment variables does the project use (`BASE_URL`, `K6_PROFILE`)?
questions.answer: BASE_URL points to TestFlow (default http://localhost:5050). K6_PROFILE selects load profile: smoke, load, stress, spike, soak, breakpoint, ci. K6_SCENARIO identifies the scenario for reports. DEMO_EMAIL/DEMO_PASSWORD and SERVICE_CLIENT_ID/SECRET for auth. K6_SOAK_MINUTES reduces soak locally. CI=true activates ci profile automatically. K6_INFLUXDB, K6_DASHBOARD control observability.
keyPoints: config/environments.js: __ENV.BASE_URL, K6_PROFILE, credentials
getProfile(): CI=true + smoke → profiles.ci (more VUs, short)
K6_SCENARIO used in buildSummary and scenario-catalog
Secrets in CI: DEMO_PASSWORD via GitHub secrets
GRAFANA_PORT, K6_WEB_DASHBOARD_PORT for local dashboard
followUps: How to point BASE_URL to staging?
Invalid K6_PROFILE — what happens?

questions.question: How do you configure credentials without hardcoding in the repository?
questions.answer: config/environments.js reads __ENV.DEMO_EMAIL, DEMO_PASSWORD, SERVICE_CLIENT_ID, and SERVICE_CLIENT_SECRET with demo defaults only for local development. Production/CI injects via .env (gitignored), GitHub Secrets (DEMO_PASSWORD in k6.yml), or runner environment variables. Never commit .env with real passwords; document .env.example without sensitive values.
keyPoints: Pattern __ENV.VAR || 'default' — override per environment
CI: secrets.DEMO_PASSWORD in k6.yml workflow
run-k6.sh sources .env if it exists (set -a)
SERVICE_CLIENT_* for OAuth client credentials if needed
Anti-pattern: logging token/password — see best practices section 18

questions.question: Does k6 browser require additional installation?
questions.answer: Yes. The browser module requires k6 v0.52+ compiled with Chromium support — it does not come in the default slim grafana/k6 Docker image. Native installation via brew/apt includes browser in recent builds. login-page.js uses import { browser } from 'k6/browser' with shared-iterations executor. In the project, browser does not run in CI smoke — local execution with native k6.
keyPoints: k6/browser: embedded Chromium — heavy vs API-only
Default Docker image: API tests only
login-page.js: 2 VUs, 4 iterations, data-testid selectors
Metrics: browser_page_load p(95)<5000, browser_lcp
CI alternative: Playwright for UI; k6 browser for local perf
followUps: How to install k6 with browser on Linux CI?

questions.question: Explain the `config/` folder (profiles, thresholds, environments).
questions.answer: config/ centralizes non-script configuration: environments.js defines BASE_URL, credentials, and TAGS; profiles.js exports stages per test type (smoke, load, stress, spike, soak, breakpoint, ci) with getProfile(); thresholds.js maps SLOs per profile via getThresholds(). Scenarios import these modules — changing SLO or load in one place reflects across the entire suite.
keyPoints: profiles.js: stages + gracefulRampDown per profile
thresholds.js: smokeThresholds, loadThresholds, stress, soak
environments.js: __ENV vars, TAGS for scenarios
getProfile(): CI smoke → ci profile automatically
Mirrored in scripts/scenario-catalog.mjs for docs/reports

questions.question: Why are endpoints centralized in `lib/endpoints.js`?
questions.answer: Single source of truth avoids scattered hardcoded URLs — a path change (/api/users) updates all scenarios at once. endpoints.js defines health, auth.login, users.list, errors, and web.* (login.html, dashboard.html, etc.). staticPages array feeds mixed-traffic. Facilitates review, scenario-catalog documentation, and alignment with testflow-cypress/playwright.
keyPoints: endpoints object: health, auth, users, errors, web
users.byId(id) for dynamic paths
staticPages: list for weighted pick in mixed-traffic
Opposite anti-pattern: literal URL in each scenario
Referenced by lib/http.js, auth.js, and journeys

questions.question: What is the role of `lib/http.js` and `lib/auth.js`?
questions.answer: lib/http.js encapsulates common requests: getHealth(), getUsers(), getStaticPage(), getErrorSimulation(), weightedPick(), and thinkTime() — each with checks and endpoint tags. lib/auth.js centralizes login(), authHeaders(), and setupAuth() for POST /api/auth/login with token validation. Scenarios stay lean; consistent checks and tags feed tag-filtered thresholds.
keyPoints: http.js: GET health/users/static + standardized checks
auth.js: login returns token or null; setupAuth for setup()
Tags: endpoint:health, endpoint:users, endpoint:auth_login
Checks: status 200, token present, non-empty users array
thinkTime/weightedPick: reusable realistic behavior

questions.question: What is the difference between `scenarios/` and `journeys/`?
questions.answer: scenarios/ contains load-type tests organized in smoke/, load/, stress/, spike/, soak/, browser/ — focus on throughput and SLOs per endpoint or mix. journeys/ models multi-step user flows with group(): login-flow.js (page → API → dashboard) and authenticated-user.js (login → users → browse). Journeys measure end-to-end duration via custom Trends (login_flow_duration, journey_duration).
keyPoints: scenarios: api-health, api-auth, mixed-traffic, api-breakpoint...
journeys: logical sequence with named group()
Same profiles/thresholds — difference is flow modeling
scenario-catalog registers both with report metadata
HTTP journeys vs browser login-page in scenarios/browser/

questions.question: What is `scripts/scenario-catalog.mjs`?
questions.answer: Node.js catalog with metadata for each scenario: script path, npm script, type (Smoke/Load/Spike...), profile, description, flow, endpoints, custom metrics, and thresholdProfile. Feeds documentation generation, HTML reports (npm run test:report), and GitHub Pages. Mirrors config/profiles.js in PROFILE_STAGES and lists SHARED_CHECKS and THRESHOLD_PROFILES for docs-code consistency.
keyPoints: SCENARIO_CATALOG: smoke, load-auth, mixed-traffic, spike, soak...

Used by build-pages-site.mjs and run-all-smoke-report.sh
Single source for PT/EN docs in site/
Avoids drift between README and actual scripts

questions.question: What does a k6 script export (`options`, `default function`, `setup`)?
questions.answer: Every k6 script exports options (executor, thresholds, scenarios configuration) and a default function (logic per iteration/VU). Optionally exports setup() (initial data), teardown(), handleSummary(), and helper functions. In api-users.js: export const options with ramping-vus; export function setup() returns token; export default function usersLoad(data) consumes token; export { handleSummary }.
keyPoints: options: required — scenarios, thresholds, tags
default function: test body per iteration
setup/teardown: lifecycle hooks once per test run
handleSummary: post-test output — lib/summary.js
Named ES module exports — import/export between files
followUps: setup vs JUnit beforeAll — analogy?

questions.question: What does `export const options` do?
questions.answer: Defines global test run configuration: scenarios (executor, stages, tags), thresholds (SLOs), browser extensions, etc. k6 reads options in the init context before spawning VUs. In mixed-traffic.js, options.scenarios.mixed_traffic uses ramping-vus executor with spread of getProfile('load') and getThresholds('load'). Changing options changes behavior without touching HTTP logic.
keyPoints: scenarios: name → { executor, stages, tags, vus, iterations }
thresholds: merge of getThresholds + custom metrics
Scenario tags propagate to filterable metrics
options.browser.type: 'chromium' in login-page.js

questions.question: What is `sleep()` for in load tests?
questions.answer: sleep() pauses VU execution for N seconds, simulating time between user actions (think time). Without sleep, VUs generate unrealistically high load — thousands of req/s that no human user produces. In api-health, sleeps of 0.5–1s between health, users, and login. Spike uses sleep(0.1) minimum; soak uses thinkTime(1, 4) for variable pauses.
keyPoints: Think time: interval between actions — realistic modeling
sleep is blocking on the VU — no CPU, goroutine yields
Load = f(VUs, script duration, sleep)
Reducing sleep increases throughput artificially
thinkTime(min, max) in lib/http.js: uniform random
followUps: sleep vs exec.vu.pauseInMs — difference?
How to calculate think time from analytics?

questions.question: What is think time and how is it simulated in testflow-k6?
questions.answer: Think time is the pause between user interactions — reading the screen, typing, deciding. In the project, thinkTime(min, max) in lib/http.js returns random seconds between min and max; mixed-traffic uses thinkTime(0.5, 2) after weightedPick; api-endurance uses thinkTime(1, 4) in soak. Values reflect real usage vs spike which minimizes pause for maximum stress.
keyPoints: thinkTime(): Math.random between min and max seconds
mixed-traffic: 0.5–2s after each weighted request
soak: 1–4s — simulates prolonged realistic usage
spike/stress: low fixed sleep (0.1–0.2s)
Adjusting think time changes RPS without changing VUs
followUps: Fixed think time vs normal distribution?

questions.question: How do you pass data between setup and VUs?
questions.answer: setup() runs once before VUs, returns a serializable object (JSON) passed as data argument to the default function. In api-users.js, setup() calls setupAuth() and returns { token }; usersLoad(data) uses data.token in getUsers(). Alternative: login() per iteration (api-auth) when each VU needs the full flow. setup is read-only shared — mutation is not safe between VUs.
keyPoints: export function setup() { return setupAuth(); }
default function(data) receives setup return value
Shared token: efficient for read-heavy users load
login per iteration: realistic for auth load and journeys
setup failure → test aborts before spawning VUs
followUps: setup vs inline login() — trade-offs?
Passing a large array in setup — limit?

questions.question: What is the `ramping-vus` executor?
questions.answer: ramping-vus adjusts the number of VUs over time according to a stages array with duration and target. Each VU executes the default function in a loop while active. It is the default executor for all API scenarios in testflow-k6: smoke, load, stress, spike, soak, and breakpoint. Stages define ramp-up, steady state, and gradual ramp-down.
keyPoints: Open-model executor: fixed VUs per stage, unlimited iterations
Spread getProfile() injects stages + gracefulRampDown
Smoke example: 10s→2 VUs, 20s steady, 5s→0
Spike: 30s→5, 10s→100, 1m steady, fast ramp-down
Alternative: shared-iterations for browser login-page
followUps: ramping-vus vs constant-vus — when to use?
How to simulate diurnal traffic with many stages?

questions.question: Explain `stages: [{ duration, target }]`.
questions.answer: Each stage defines for how long (duration, e.g. '1m', '30s') k6 maintains or ramps to target VUs. Between stages, k6 linearly interpolates the number of VUs. In load profile: 1m→10, 3m→25, 2m→25, 1m→0. duration accepts 's', 'm', 'h'. target: 0 ends VUs (ramp-down). gracefulRampDown lets active iterations finish before kill.
keyPoints: duration: string with unit — '10s', '30m'
target: integer number of VUs at end of stage
Linear ramp between consecutive stage targets
target: 0 in last stage — orderly shutdown
profiles.js: single reusable stages source
followUps: Stage with target lower than previous — ramp-down?
Total test duration = sum of stages?

questions.question: What is `gracefulRampDown`?
questions.answer: gracefulRampDown defines tolerance time after a stage with target:0 for VUs to complete in-progress iterations before shutting down. Avoids cutting HTTP requests mid-flight. In smoke, gracefulRampDown: '5s'; load uses '30s'; stress '1m'. Without it, final metrics may have aborted requests and false negative checks.
keyPoints: String duration — e.g. '5s', '30s', '1m'
Applies when VUs are reduced or zeroed
Important in long journeys with multiple HTTP calls

questions.question: What is the difference between `ramping-vus` and `shared-iterations`?
questions.answer: ramping-vus: VUs execute default function in a loop for duration — open model, common in load/stress. shared-iterations: fixed total iterations divided among VUs — closed model. login-page.js uses shared-iterations with vus:2, iterations:4 (2 iter/VU) because browser is heavy. questions.question: What is the difference between `check()` and `thresholds`?
questions.answer: check() is a per-response assertion — status 200, token present — records pass/fail in the checks metric without immediately aborting the iteration. thresholds are aggregated gates at the end: e.g. checks: ['rate>0.99'], http_req_duration: ['p(95)<1500']. If a threshold fails, k6 exit code ≠ 0. In testflow-k6, checks live in lib/http.js and auth.js; thresholds in config/thresholds.js.

threshold: aggregated, p95/p99/rate, determines CI pass/fail

questions.question: What happens when a threshold fails?
questions.answer: k6 completes the test run, aggregates final metrics, and evaluates thresholds. Any violation results in non-zero exit code (typically 1), failing the CI job. Console shows ✗ next to the violated threshold. In k6.yml, the 'Run k6 smoke' step fails the PR if p95 or checks rate violate smokeThresholds. Summary artifact is still uploaded with if: always().

questions.question: Explain `http_req_duration: ['p(95)<1500']`.
questions.answer: Requires 95% of HTTP requests to complete in less than 1500 ms. p(95) is the 95th percentile — the slowest 5% may exceed it. smokeThresholds uses global p(95)<1500 and per-endpoint filters (health p95<500). load relaxes to p(95)<2500. Unit is milliseconds. Built-in http_req_duration metric aggregates all HTTP requests in the test.

questions.question: What does `checks: ['rate>0.99']` mean?
questions.answer: Threshold on the checks metric requires check() success rate above 99% — at most 1% of assertions may fail. smoke and soak use rate>0.99; load accepts rate>0.98; stress rate>0.95. checks rate is aggregated from all check() in the test: health status 200, login token present, etc.

questions.question: What is the goal of a soak/endurance test?

questions.question: Why does Grafana not run on GitHub Pages?
questions.answer: GitHub Pages serves static files (HTML/CSS/JS) — no backend, database, or WebSocket for InfluxDB queries. Grafana requires a server with live datasource. Solution: embed Grafana Cloud iframe (GRAFANA_EMBED vars) in static site via write-grafana-config.mjs, or external link to hosted dashboard. Pages HTML report uses pre-generated JSON summaries, not live Grafana.

report/index.html: client-side charts from exported JSON

Local dev: docker compose monitoring profile

questions.question: What runs in the `k6.yml` workflow?
questions.answer: Three jobs: test (smoke gate on PR/push — api-health with CI=true, qaschool/testflow:5050 service, upload summary artifact); load-manual (workflow_dispatch — mixed-traffic with chosen profile); publish-pages (push main — build site, npm run test:report, deploy GitHub Pages). Installs k6 via apt, uses secrets.DEMO_PASSWORD, timeout 10–30 min.

Service container: qaschool/testflow health-checked

publish-pages: permissions pages:write, id-token: write
concurrency cancel-in-progress per ref

questions.question: What runs in the `pages.yml` workflow?

questions.question: How do you use `workflow_dispatch` for manual load?
questions.answer: In k6.yml, on.workflow_dispatch exposes input profile (smoke/load/stress/spike). load-manual job runs if event_name == workflow_dispatch — runs mixed-traffic.js with K6_PROFILE=${{ inputs.profile }}. Timeout 30 min, upload artifact k6-summary-{profile}. Lets QA run load on demand without merge — GitHub Actions UI → Run workflow → choose profile.

questions.question: Which secret is required in CI?
questions.answer: DEMO_PASSWORD — password for TestFlow demo user for POST /api/auth/login. Referenced as secrets.DEMO_PASSWORD in test, load-manual, and publish-pages jobs. questions.question: k6 vs JMeter — pros and cons?
questions.answer: k6: versionable JS scripts, low Go footprint, native thresholds, CI-friendly — used in testflow-k6. JMeter: mature GUI, huge plugin ecosystem, legacy protocols (SOAP, JDBC), heavy JVM threads. k6 better for devops/modern API; JMeter for Java enterprise teams with existing XML investment. testflow-k6 chooses k6 for JS integration with Cypress/Playwright.
keyPoints: k6: goroutines, exit code thresholds, Git-native
JMeter: .jmx GUI, costly threads, complex reports
k6 lower learning curve for JS devs
JMeter: recording proxy, plugin extensibility
Both open source — k6 Cloud vs BlazeMeter JMeter
followUps: Migrate JMeter .jmx to k6?
JMeter DSL Java vs k6 JS?

questions.question: k6 vs Gatling?
questions.answer: Gatling uses Scala/Kotlin DSL with excellent HTML reports and sophisticated traffic modeling. k6 uses JavaScript with lower curve for web QAs and natural integration with TestFlow JS stack. Gatling JVM consumes more resources; k6 Go engine is lighter. Gatling popular in Scala/Java backend; k6 in startups and GitHub Actions pipelines.
keyPoints: Gatling: Scala DSL, rich built-in reports
k6: JS, Grafana ecosystem, xk6 extensions
Gatling: advanced closed/open model
k6: SLO-first thresholds
Choice often dictated by team language preference
followUps: Gatling front-end vs k6 — comparable?

questions.question: k6 vs Locust?
questions.answer: Locust uses Python — @task decorators, web swarm UI, popular in Python/Django teams. k6 JavaScript aligns with testflow-cypress/playwright JS. Locust greenlets vs k6 goroutines — both efficient. k6 thresholds and handleSummary more integrated for CI gates. Locust flexible for complex Python logic; k6 for unified JS pipeline.
keyPoints: Locust: Python, web UI swarm control
k6: JS, CLI-first, Grafana integration

questions.question: When to choose k6 Cloud vs self-hosted?

Project demonstrates complete self-hosted; Cloud for teams without ops capacity or geo-distributed tests.

questions.question: How do you interpret p50, p95, p99?
questions.answer: Percentiles indicate latency below which X% of requests completed. p50 (median): typical experience. p95: almost everyone except outliers — common SLO (smoke p95<1500). p99: long tail — most affected users. smokeThresholds requires p95<1500 and p99<5000 global. Never use average alone — hides critical outliers.
keyPoints: p50: half of requests faster than this value
p95: 95% below — main testflow-k6 smoke SLO
p99: tail — detect severe outliers
avg<800 smoke — complementary, not substitute for p95
Minimum samples: smoke CI may have few datapoints
followUps: p99 unstable with few samples?
p95 vs customer-facing SLA?

questions.question: What does high `http_req_failed` indicate?
questions.answer: Elevated rate of failed requests — timeouts, connection refused, HTTP 5xx, or status 0. In smoke, threshold rate<0.005 (0.5%). Causes: TestFlow down (localhost:5050), overload under stress, invalid credentials (DEMO_PASSWORD), Docker network (host.docker.internal). CI verifies curl /health before k6. spike/stress tolerate up to 5% failed.
keyPoints: http_req_failed: rate of unsuccessful requests
Status 0: timeout, connection reset, DNS fail
Smoke: <0.5% — near zero tolerance

Correlate with checks rate and TestFlow logs

questions.question: How do you identify bottleneck between auth and users?
questions.answer: Compare http_req_duration{endpoint:auth_login} vs {endpoint:users} in smoke/load thresholds. api-auth isolates login with login_duration Trend; api-users isolates GET /api/users with setup token. If auth_login p95>3000 and users p95<800, bottleneck is authentication. mixed-traffic weighted pick mixes both — use tags to filter in summary JSON or Grafana.
keyPoints: Tags endpoint:auth_login vs endpoint:users
Dedicated scenarios: api-auth.js vs api-users.js
login_duration p(95)<2000 auth threshold

questions.question: What to analyze in `degraded_responses` during stress?

questions.question: Should you log tokens or passwords in k6?
questions.answer: Never. console.log(token) or password appear in public CI logs, artifacts, and Grafana. testflow-k6 uses __ENV for credentials — does not print login payload. GitHub Actions logs are visible to the team. If debug needed, log only hash/truncated or use ephemeral local env. secrets.DEMO_PASSWORD never echoed in workflow.

questions.question: How do you propose SLOs for a new endpoint?
questions.answer: Baseline: run smoke api-health after deploy in staging, measure current p95 without load. Load test api-users or dedicated scenario under load profile. Add threshold in config/thresholds.js with endpoint:name tag and 20% margin above baseline. Align with Cypress E2E timeout. Document in scenario-catalog THRESHOLD_PROFILES. Iterate after production feedback.
keyPoints: 1. Measure baseline p95/p99 in smoke
2. Load test — observe degradation
3. threshold = baseline * 1.2 under load
4. Tag endpoint in lib/http.js helper
5. PR with thresholds.js + scenario if needed
followUps: Too aggressive SLO — CI flake?

questions.question: Product asks for 10,000 VUs — how do you respond realistically?
questions.answer: Educate about cost, infra, and objective: 10k VUs requires k6 Cloud cluster or distributed fleet — not laptop or GitHub Actions. Ask: expected real traffic? RPS target? questions.question: How would you explain threshold to a non-technical stakeholder?
questions.answer: Threshold is an automatic pass/fail target — e.g. '99% of logins complete in less than 2 seconds'. Analogy: passing grade on a test. If violated, pipeline alerts before users complain. testflow-k6 CI smoke: 'app is fast enough to merge'. Avoid p95 jargon initially — use '95 out of every 100 requests'.
keyPoints: Analogy: red line / minimum grade
Concrete example: login < 2s for 99% of users
Benefit: detect slowness before production
CI smoke gate — business language: quality gate
Relate to user experience, not technical ms

questions.question: Which metric would you prioritize: p95, throughput, or error rate?
questions.answer: Depends on context — mature answer recognizes trade-offs. UX-facing APIs: p95 latency first (smokeThresholds p95<1500). High availability: error rate (http_req_failed<0.01). Capacity planning: throughput (http_reqs/s) + breakpoint. testflow-k6 prioritizes p95 + error rate + checks rate in CI smoke. Throughput alone ignores slow users.
keyPoints: p95: typical user experience under load
error rate: reliability — smoke <0.5%
throughput: capacity — stress/breakpoint context
Project: checks rate>0.99 + p95 + failed rate trio
Product SLI defines priority — not dogma

questions.question: At which CI pipeline stage does performance testing fit?
questions.answer: Perf smoke on every PR — k6.yml test job runs api-health before merge (~1 min gate). Load/stress manual post-merge or workflow_dispatch — does not block PR. publish-pages on main generates historical report. Shift-left light model + scheduled deep test. Aligned with testflow-k6: fail fast smoke, heavy load on demand.
keyPoints: PR: k6 smoke gate — early perf regression
Main: Pages report + artifact history
Load: manual workflow_dispatch — QA trigger
Optional nightly: soak/stress in dedicated env
Does not replace unit/E2E — additional layer
